category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Linear Algebra — Characteristic Polynomial and Eigenvalues

questiontext.text: The eigenvalues of \(A=\begin{pmatrix}4&1\\2&3\end{pmatrix}\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 2 and 5

Characteristic polynomial: \(\det(A-\lambda I)=\lambda^2-7\lambda+10=(\lambda-2)(\lambda-5)=0\). Check: trace\(=4+3=7\) ✓, det\(=12-2=10\) ✓. Eigenvalues: \(\lambda=2,5\).

questiontext.text: The Cayley–Hamilton theorem states that every matrix satisfies:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Its own characteristic polynomial

Cayley–Hamilton: if \(p(\lambda)=\det(\lambda I-A)\) is the characteristic polynomial of \(A\), then \(p(A)=0\). For \(A=\begin{pmatrix}4&1\\2&3\end{pmatrix}\): \(p(\lambda)=\lambda^2-7\lambda+10\), so \(A^2-7A+10I=0\). This allows expressing \(A^{-1}=\frac{1}{10}(7I-A)\).

questiontext.text: For a \(2\times2\) matrix \(A\) with characteristic polynomial \(P_A(\lambda)=\lambda^2-s\lambda+d\) (where \(s=\mathrm{tr}(A)\), \(d=\det A\)), which is TRUE about \(P_A(\lambda)-P_{A^{-1}}(\lambda)\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) It is a polynomial of degree \(\leq1\) (not constant in general)

\(P_{A^{-1}}(\lambda)=\lambda^2-s'\lambda+d'\) where \(s'=\mathrm{tr}(A^{-1})\) and \(d'=1/d\). These differ from \(s,d\) in general, so \(P_A-P_{A^{-1}}\) is a degree-2 polynomial, not necessarily constant. Only if \(A\) is orthogonal (\(A^{-1}=A^T\)) would there be a simpler relationship.

answer.text: It is always a nonzero constant
It depends on \(A\) and is not constant in general
It equals the minimal polynomial of \(A\)

questiontext.text: If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A\) with eigenvector \(v\), then the eigenvalue of \(A^k\) corresponding to \(v\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\lambda^k\)

If \(Av=\lambda v\), then \(A^2v=A(\lambda v)=\lambda Av=\lambda^2v\), and by induction \(A^kv=\lambda^kv\). So eigenvalues of \(A^k\) are \(k\)-th powers of eigenvalues of \(A\). This generalises: if \(p\) is a polynomial, \(p(A)v=p(\lambda)v\).

questiontext.text: For an \(n\times n\) matrix \(A\), the sum of all eigenvalues equals \(\mathrm{tr}(A)\) and the product equals \(\det(A)\). For \(A=\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\3&4\end{pmatrix}\), the eigenvalues sum to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) 5

\(\mathrm{tr}(A)=1+4=5=\lambda_1+\lambda_2\). \(\det(A)=4-6=-2=\lambda_1\lambda_2\). Characteristic polynomial: \(\lambda^2-5\lambda-2=0\), giving \(\lambda=(5\pm\sqrt{33})/2\). Sum \(=5\), product \(=-2\).

questiontext.text: All eigenvalues of a real symmetric matrix are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Real

Spectral theorem: a real symmetric matrix has all real eigenvalues and is orthogonally diagonalisable. Proof: if \(Av=\lambda v\), then \(\bar\lambda\|v\|^2=\langle Av,v\rangle=\langle v,Av\rangle=\lambda\|v\|^2\), so \(\bar\lambda=\lambda\), i.e., \(\lambda\in\mathbb{R}\). They need not be distinct or positive.

questiontext.text: The eigenvalues of an orthogonal matrix \(Q\) (with \(Q^TQ=I\)) satisfy:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(|\lambda|=1\)

If \(Qv=\lambda v\), then \(\|v\|^2=\|Qv\|^2=|\lambda|^2\|v\|^2\), so \(|\lambda|=1\). Real orthogonal matrices have eigenvalues \(\pm1\) or complex conjugate pairs \(e^{\pm i\theta}\) on the unit circle.

questiontext.text: For \(A=\begin{pmatrix}0&0&1\\1&0&0\\0&1&0\end{pmatrix}\), the characteristic polynomial is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(\lambda^3-1\)

\(A\) is a permutation matrix (cyclic permutation). \(\det(\lambda I-A)=\lambda^3-1\) (expand along first row). The eigenvalues are the cube roots of unity: \(1, \omega, \omega^2\) where \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}\).

questiontext.text: The geometric multiplicity of an eigenvalue \(\lambda\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\dim\ker(A-\lambda I)\)

Geometric multiplicity = dimension of the eigenspace = \(\dim\ker(A-\lambda I)\). Algebraic multiplicity = multiplicity of \(\lambda\) as a root of the characteristic polynomial. Always: geometric \(\leq\) algebraic. Equality for all eigenvalues \(\Leftrightarrow\) diagonalisable.

answer.text: The multiplicity of \(\lambda\) as a root of the characteristic polynomial
The number of distinct eigenvalues of \(A\)

questiontext.text: If \(A\) is nilpotent (\(A^k=0\) for some \(k\)), then all eigenvalues of \(A\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) 0

If \(Av=\lambda v\) and \(A^k=0\), then \(0=A^kv=\lambda^kv\), so \(\lambda^k=0\), hence \(\lambda=0\). Conversely, a matrix over \(\mathbb{C}\) is nilpotent iff all its eigenvalues are 0 (by Jordan form). The characteristic polynomial of a nilpotent matrix is \(\lambda^n\).

questiontext.text: If \(A^2=A\) (idempotent / projection), the eigenvalues of \(A\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Only 0 and 1

If \(Av=\lambda v\), then \(A^2v=\lambda^2v=Av=\lambda v\), so \(\lambda^2=\lambda\), giving \(\lambda(\lambda-1)=0\), i.e., \(\lambda=0\) or \(\lambda=1\). The matrix \(A\) is diagonalisable (over \(\mathbb{R}\)) with diagonal entries 0 and 1, corresponding to the projection onto the range along the kernel.

questiontext.text: The eigenvalues of the companion matrix \(C=\begin{pmatrix}0&0&-c_0\\1&0&-c_1\\0&1&-c_2\end{pmatrix}\) are the roots of:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\lambda^3+c_2\lambda^2+c_1\lambda+c_0\)

The characteristic polynomial of the companion matrix of \(p(\lambda)=\lambda^n+c_{n-1}\lambda^{n-1}+\cdots+c_0\) is exactly \(p(\lambda)\). This gives a matrix whose eigenvalues are the roots of any given monic polynomial — fundamental in linear systems theory.

answer.text: Cannot be determined from entries alone

questiontext.text: By Gershgorin's theorem, every eigenvalue of \(A=\begin{pmatrix}5&1&0\\0&3&2\\1&0&7\end{pmatrix}\) lies in the union of discs:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(|\lambda-5|\leq1\), \(|\lambda-3|\leq2\), \(|\lambda-7|\leq1\)

Gershgorin: each eigenvalue lies in at least one disc \(|\lambda-a_{ii}|\leq\sum_{j\neq i}|a_{ij}|\). Row 1: centre 5, radius \(|1|+|0|=1\). Row 2: centre 3, radius \(|0|+|2|=2\). Row 3: centre 7, radius \(|1|+|0|=1\).

questiontext.text: If \(B=P^{-1}AP\), then \(A\) and \(B\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Have the same characteristic polynomial and eigenvalues

\(\det(\lambda I-B)=\det(\lambda I-P^{-1}AP)=\det(P^{-1}(\lambda I-A)P)=\det(\lambda I-A)\). So similar matrices have the same characteristic polynomial, hence the same eigenvalues (with the same algebraic multiplicities), same trace, and same determinant.

answer.text: Have the same characteristic polynomial and eigenvalues

questiontext.text: If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A\), then the eigenvalues of \(A+cI\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\lambda+c\)

If \(Av=\lambda v\), then \((A+cI)v=Av+cv=\lambda v+cv=(\lambda+c)v\). So \(v\) is an eigenvector of \(A+cI\) with eigenvalue \(\lambda+c\). Adding a scalar multiple of the identity shifts all eigenvalues by \(c\).

questiontext.text: A real symmetric matrix \(A\) is positive definite if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) All eigenvalues are positive

By the spectral theorem, a real symmetric matrix is orthogonally diagonalisable: \(A=Q\Lambda Q^T\). Then \(x^TAx=x^TQ\Lambda Q^Tx=y^T\Lambda y=\sum\lambda_iy_i^2\). This is positive for all \(x\neq0\) iff all \(\lambda_i>0\). Equivalent: all leading principal minors positive (Sylvester's criterion).

name.text: Eigenvalues - Eigenvalues of AB vs BA

questiontext.text: For square matrices \(A\) and \(B\) of the same size, the nonzero eigenvalues of \(AB\) and \(BA\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) The same (nonzero eigenvalues of \(AB\) = nonzero eigenvalues of \(BA\))

If \(\lambda eq0\) is an eigenvalue of \(AB\) with \(ABv=\lambda v\), then \(BA(Bv)=B(AB)v=\lambda Bv\), and \(Bv eq0\) (since \(Bv=0\Rightarrow ABv=0\), contradicting \(\lambda eq0\)). So \(\lambda\) is also an eigenvalue of \(BA\). The argument is symmetric. Same for rectangular matrices.

questiontext.text: The minimal polynomial of \(A=\begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&2\end{pmatrix}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\lambda-2\)

\(A=2I\), so \(A-2I=0\). The minimal polynomial divides every annihilating polynomial. Since \(A-2I=0\), the minimal polynomial is \(\lambda-2\) (degree 1). The characteristic polynomial is \((\lambda-2)^2\). For scalar matrices \(cI\), the minimal polynomial is always \(\lambda-c\).

questiontext.text: The spectral radius \(\rho(A)=\max|\lambda_i|\) of \(A=\begin{pmatrix}0&2\\2&0\end{pmatrix}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 2

Eigenvalues: \(\det(\lambda I-A)=\lambda^2-4=0\Rightarrow\lambda=\pm2\). Spectral radius \(=\max(|2|,|-2|)=2\). For a symmetric matrix, the spectral radius equals \(\|A\|_2\) (operator norm). Also \(\rho(A)\leq\|A\|\) for any matrix norm.

questiontext.text: A \(2\times2\) real matrix \(A\) has \(\mathrm{tr}(A)=5\) and \(\det(A)=6\). The eigenvalues are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 2 and 3

Characteristic polynomial: \(\lambda^2-5\lambda+6=0\Rightarrow(\lambda-2)(\lambda-3)=0\). Eigenvalues are 2 and 3. Check: \(2+3=5\) ✓, \(2\times3=6\) ✓.
